title: Cybersecurity Systems
entry_mechanism: Saudi cybersecurity companies developing defence-specific security products through PSAP Track 1
executive_summary:
Saudi Arabia's defence infrastructure — command systems, logistics platforms, communications networks, and weapon system controllers — is a high-value cyber target. The 2012 Shamoon attack on Saudi Aramco (which destroyed 35,000 computers) demonstrated that Saudi Arabia faces sophisticated state-level cyber threats. Defence-specific cybersecurity — network intrusion detection, endpoint protection for classified networks, and cryptographic systems — is today almost entirely procured from US and Israeli vendors, creating a supply chain risk.

Node DE-2 targets the development of Saudi-origin defence cybersecurity products: network monitoring tools adapted for Saudi military network architectures, Arabic-language threat intelligence platforms, and endpoint protection systems that do not require foreign OEM access to Saudi military network data. The cryptographic element — sovereign encryption for classified communications — is the highest-value target but requires GADD co-development given its classification implications.

Saudi Arabia already has a growing commercial cybersecurity industry — companies like Cysiv, Cyberani (Aramco Digital subsidiary), and Elm's security division have meaningful technical capability. The gap is defence-grade product development: software that meets military certification standards, handles classified data, and operates in air-gapped environments.

companies.name: Rohde & Schwarz Cybersecurity
companies.rationale: R&S Cybersecurity develops network encryption and security products for German and NATO defence networks. Non-ITAR technology with established Saudi presence through R&S communications programmes.

companies.name: Thales Cybersecurity
companies.rationale: Thales is Europe's leading defence cryptography and cybersecurity company. Non-ITAR French technology; active Gulf government engagement; HSM and encryption products.
companies.contact: Thales Cybersecurity VP Government, Middle East

companies.name: CrowdStrike
companies.rationale: CrowdStrike has established Saudi Arabia government relationships and a Saudi data residency programme. Could support a Saudi-developed endpoint protection product through technology licensing.

companies.name: BAE Systems Applied Intelligence
companies.rationale: BAE Applied Intelligence develops military cyber defence tools. Active Saudi Arabia engagement through existing BAE programmes.
companies.contact: BAE Applied Intelligence VP Cyber, Middle East

companies.name: Cisco Systems / Cisco Defence
companies.rationale: Cisco has Saudi Arabia data residency and government relationships. Cisco's network security APIs support Saudi-developed security applications on top of Cisco infrastructure.

partners.name: Cyberani (Aramco Digital)
partners.current: OT cybersecurity for industrial control systems; Arabic threat intelligence; SOC operations
partners.upgrade: Defence-grade product development; NSCS clearance for classified network security products; GADD-co-funded military network monitoring tool

partners.name: NCA (National Cybersecurity Authority)
partners.current: National cybersecurity standards; threat intelligence; government network security
partners.upgrade: NCA-GADD defence cybersecurity product co-development; Saudi military cybersecurity certification framework; national defence cyber threat intelligence platform

partners.name: Elm Company / Elm Security
partners.current: Government IT security; secure platform development; identity management
partners.upgrade: Defence endpoint protection product; military network anomaly detection; NSCS clearance

partners.name: KACST Cybersecurity Research
partners.current: Cryptography research; network security; some AI-based threat detection research
partners.upgrade: GADD-KACST defence cryptography programme; Saudi-origin encryption algorithm research; military network anomaly detection research
